speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four with Hilde Wolf Jaffe. The date is August 3, 1995. And the interviewer is Amy Margolis Cohen. OK, you were explaining how you quickly left from Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We met in Cologne-- my sister, my mother, my father, and I. And we had a little time, I remember, to go to the train station. Across the street from the train station, there was a-- right near there, there was a store, a jewelry store. And my father bought a ring for my mother with the last money that he had, that he couldn't take out of Germany. So my mother had this ring. And we got on the train. And, somehow, my father was very calm through all this, because there had been several telephone calls to the-- the relatives, where he was staying. But he was never home. And they didn't find him. He never went back. We got on the train and the train was headed for Brussels, where we had friends. We had friends staying there who used to live in Germany, but they had immigrated to Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got to the border. And the Nazis came through to examine our papers-- the passports and everything. Well, they were in order. It seems that the travel bureau-- the United States Lines travel-- it was an American travel agency that my father had dealt with in Cologne-- had made all the arrangements so that we had visas to go into Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was all done within a number of hours, because it was-- overnight, we were gone. And we got through the border, and I remember I wanted some cookies. They were vending cookies on the platform. And I carried-- this is a sign of how little impressed I was with the danger of what was going on. And, finally, my mother bought me the cookies. I got these Aachener printen, which are a specialty. You can only get in Aachen, which is the border town. Well, the people eventually left and the train pulled out of the station in Aachen, because there was a long stop, because everybody's papers got examined. And as the train pulled out of the station, my father sat down in a corner of our compartment and collapsed. And he started to cry. I had never seen my father cry before. He just went totally to pieces. And after a while, he got himself together, and he said, you know, we've done it. We're out of Germany. We made it. We're all together and we're safe. And that was the beginning of my life as a refugee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which I will ask you about in one minute. So you only had that one grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your grandmother and all these aunts and uncles and cousins were still left in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all left in Germany at that point, except for my mother's sister, Aunt Erna. She was here. She's the one who got us out of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left your home--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything that was critically important to you to make sure you took with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a doll. I was allowed to bring one toy. And I brought this doll. It was a beautiful doll. My father had bought it for me-- one of the very few excursions into the city that I went with my father, alone. And he put me on the train, back to Surth, all by myself. That was my first train trip alone. I must have been six years old. And I was very, very fond of that doll. One of the most tragic things of leaving-- what really hurt me the most as a child-- was the fact that we couldn't take our dog, [NON-ENGLISH]. This was a wonderful dog. He was a big Great Dane. And he and I grew up together. He was a puppy when I was born. And I was 10 years old when I left Germany. And the dog was 10 years old, but you couldn't take a Great Dane with us on our travels. No way. And when we moved out of the house, just before Christmas-- you know, at the time that we left Surth, The dog was picked up. And that was the end of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was picked up by whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father made arrangements. And we were all terribly sad. My father loved the dog. But I loved the dog, I think, more than anybody, because he was like my friend. He-- It was wonderful. I just loved that dog. So that was sad. That was really sad. When I think of it, I still feel very, very sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was one of your worst moments of that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. As a child, these are the things that touch you-- losing the dog. So anyway, we arrived in Brussels. And my father's friends got us a room in a rooming house somewhere, where we stayed for about two weeks. And I remember it as a relatively pleasant stay. I mean, we were with friends. We ate there a lot, at the friend's house. And I remember going shopping and watching my mother try to maneuver without knowing the language, because we-- was French and we didn't speak French. But she managed somehow. And then, it was decided that we would leave Brussels a week early, before the ship was ready to leave from Le Havre, because our passage had been changed from Hamburg to Le Havre. And we would spend that week with my mother's aunt in Paris. And that was the first time I saw Paris. And that was terribly exciting. I remember the aunt taking us to see the stores and those broad boulevards. And, you know, the child's view of Paris-- and one thing I remember that she took us for lunch or dinner at some restaurant, which had a roof garden. We were on a roof somewhere, looking at Paris. And then, of course, they had funny toilets in France in those days. They didn't have regular toilets, like I knew from Germany-- where you had to crouch down low. That was something that I had never seen before. So things were going pretty well. And then, of course, there was the ship-- the boat-- the When we got on there, that was child's paradise. I loved the idea of eating all our meals in that beautiful dining room, with the waiter taking care of us. We were not the kind of family that ate out a lot in restaurants. So, to me, the passage was absolute luxury. It was just very, very exciting. It was very cold. I remember a lot of people got seasick. This was because there was some kind of a storm. And they weren't even-- we weren't allowed on deck. My sister and I sneaked up on the deck. And there was ice all over the deck, so we were sliding, you know, the way kids do on ice, and had a wonderful time until they chased us off the deck, because they were afraid we'd fall overboard or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the other passengers on the boat? Were they also Jews escaping from the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some. But they were also people who were just-- you know, Americans were still traveling. And they were mostly Americans on the boat. The food wasn't exactly what we were used to. But it was good. It was wonderful. There was plenty of everything. We thought orange juice-- that was a discovery. We used to eat oranges. But we didn't have orange juice. That was something new. We drank a lot orange juice on the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was all actually a pleasant experience for you. And it's not like you felt this enormous relief of getting out of Germany, because you had been quite protected.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had been protected. To me, it was a great experience. The difficulties, for me personally, were about to start here. The first few years in New York were really rough.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had to start school. And my aunt had rented an apartment for us in Astoria, where she was actually working. She had a day job there, being a maid in somebody else's house. And she needed a place to stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she rented a five room apartment for us. There were three bedrooms. My parents had one, my sister and I shared one, and my Aunt Erna had the third bedroom. She was like a boarder in the house. Then there was a kitchen, which you could eat in, and a living room. It was a railroad flat. It was nothing fancy.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we lived there. And we were quite-- you know, we were happy to be there. But my mother got sick. My mother was sick and sicker. So Aunt Rose told her to go-- we didn't have any money here, except what we brought from-- the money we had transferred by way of Switzerland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was some money, but my parents were afraid to touch that. They were really afraid. This stood between us and starvation, and the last thing we would have been-- even though we had the aunt and uncle vouch for us, that they were going to take care of us-- we never, ever wanted to be taken care of by anybody. My parents were very proud.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were going to take care of themselves, no matter what. So that money was-- only in the direst of emergencies were we to touch that. Well, my father got a job right away. It was the depression. It was 1938. We got here February '38. He got a job as a dishwasher down near the fish market in some restaurant. And he worked there. And I still don't know how we made ends meet, but I guess Aunt Erna's contribution made it, because we paid $42 a month in rent. And my father earned $12 a week. So you figure that one out. My mother did home work. She started doing home work. And that was making little bows for the Greek Orthodox Church. People used to pin those little pink or blue bows on, with little medallions-- little religious medallions and crosses-- at christenings and wedding. They used to put them on their lapel. And we made those bows. And both my sister and I helped my mother after school. So, that way, I suppose we made ends meet-- between the home work and the boarder that we had in the house, my aunt. But let me get back to going to school. The first day in school, my aunt-- was Aunt Rose, my mother's aunt. There was Aunt Rose and Uncle Ike and my aunt Erna, which was my mother's sister. But Aunt Erna worked all day. She had this job as a housekeeper, as a maid.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, we went to school. Aunt Erna made sure that she rented this apartment in the same block as the elementary school, PS 5 in Astoria. That's where I went to school. And they put me in a classroom. I didn't know what people were saying because I couldn't understand a word of English.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I sat in that classroom. And I sat and I sat. And they were talking and they were doing work. But I didn't know what was going on. I couldn't understand anybody. And evidently, the teacher knew this-- that I didn't understand a word of what was being said-- so she figured I'm Jewish. Her name was Ms. Kaminski. She wasn't Jewish. But I guess she was Polish or something like that. But she was an American woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she figured, if I'm Jewish, if there's somebody else who speaks Jewish, they would be able to talk to me. So she asked which of the children, if anybody spoke Jewish, which would be Yiddish. How did she even know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my aunt must have explained that I just came from Germany and I was Jewish, you know, and that I'm a refugee, and that I don't speak English. So anyway, this girl came over and she starts speaking Yiddish to me. And I didn't understand her. And I started to cry. And I was so unhappy, because here was this other girl my age, trying to talk to me. And I couldn't understand. I couldn't understand anything. Well, that morning finally came to an end and I went home for lunch. And I told my mother, I am not going back. I can't learn anything in that school anyway. And I hate it. And I am not going back to that school. No way. Well, I don't know what my mother did. I know she didn't beat me. And she didn't yell at me. And she fed me lunch. But I went back to school that afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed in school. And I took with me a little dictionary, a little German-English Dictionary. And I think I had the teacher or anybody, if they really wanted to tell me something, look up the English word, and then point to the German. Somehow, the dictionary is what got me through those first few days. I mean, I didn't really learn anything. But I learned a few English words. And that was the beginning. This was in February. Somehow, by the end of June, I spoke English. I don't know how. I don't know how I learned. Somehow, I started to speak. I know I spoke with an accent and my vocabulary wasn't what it should have been. But I was a reader. And I loved to read. I was used to reading, because I was reading many German books-- children's books. And I even had a few with me. But I knew that I couldn't read German books anymore. Somewhere, I heard that one of the best books for little girls was Louisa May Alcott. And that if you want books for nothing, this is a wonderful country. You can get books out of the library. So I got out of the library. And I went through that with the German dictionary. I will tell you, by the time I got to the middle of the book, I didn't remember what was in the beginning of the book. By the time I got to the end, I didn't remember what was in the middle. Because looking up every word that I didn't understand was-- it took me forever. A few years later, I read the book again to just find out what it was all about. But that's how I learned English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed in school and went to high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in school. And I had some very good experiences because, first of all, they didn't put me back in the first grade. I was put in first grade-- in fifth grade, when I came-- which was the same grade that I left in Germany. It made it very difficult for me. I was just like one half year behind my normal place, where I should have been according to my age. And the following year, in Germany, in match, you do decimals first. Here, you did regular fractions before decimals. And that stood me in good stead. Because when we got around to doing decimals in the second half of fifth grade, I told the teacher, I already know this. And, you know, I really-- I know this-- all of this. And I showed to her that I knew it. So they skipped me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I was with my normal group of children. And that was fine. So school was fine. School was fine. I had no friends at first. I mean, who wanted to be friends with this funny looking refugee kid that wore all these German clothes? But I think that's what made me get to be a good student.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, nobody knew my sister. I didn't have to live up to her standards anymore. I was on my own. And I really wanted to learn and I had nothing much to distract me. And I really studied. I did all my assignments. And I really became a good student. The teachers liked me, which didn't always endear me to my fellow classmates, because I had that feeling that sometimes that was difficult. In the meantime, at home, things went from bad to worse. My mother was-- went to Mt. Sinai Hospital. She was operated on three times, until they finally got this colitis this thing under control. And over the next three years or so, she was constantly in and out of hospital. Aunt Erna lived with us and she worked very hard at her job. And she was very short-tempered with us, with my sister and me. She expected us to do housework, which we weren't used to doing, because, at home, there was always some-- in Germany-- there was always someone who helped. So it was tough, the first few years. I wasn't a happy child, even though I lost myself in school work, and I read a great deal, and I had a very vivid imagination. Every night, I used to imagine myself someplace else. It wasn't happy at home. My father missed my mother when she wasn't home. He worked very, very hard, and was usually tired when he came home at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family have contact with your relatives in Germany and hear what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had contact with them the first year. We wrote regularly. That's when I got this picture-- when we got pictures still from Germany, after we had left. And there was a wedding. One of my cousins got married. Then, after that, we didn't hear from them anymore. You know, after 1940, '41, we just didn't hear from people anymore. And we didn't know what happened. They were deported to Poland, I think. Some were deported to Riga. This, I found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find this out and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I have-- my second cousin, by the name of Otto Marx, which is-- which is not on my list of immediate family. But he and I are pretty close here. He lives in the United States. And he has done a study of the Marx's family tree. I have another second cousin-- you know, these are more distant relatives in Israel-- by the name of Thea, Thea Wolf. And she did a family tree study of the Wolfs. So the Marxs, because that was my paternal grandmother's maiden name, and the Wolfs, I have family, extensive family trees. They have both gone to Germany and checked out records, and who went where, especially my cousin Thea in Israel. She has done an enormous amount of research, and found out where people from various sections were taken. So that's how I found out, through Thea actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many years later.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't get to meet Thea myself, or even in any way have any contact with her, until 1980 when we went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that when you learned about the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew they didn't return because we never heard from them. We knew about Michael, my cousin Edith's son, that he survived in Israel. Because he had been in contact with one of the cousins, Kurt, who was in Sao Paolo in Brazil.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their last names? What are all these people's last names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their last names are either Wolf or Marx.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael.

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael's last name-- he changed his name. Well, Michael's mother was my cousin. And she married someone by the name of Sternschein. That's S-T-E-R-N-S-C-H-E-I-N. And she and her husband were both killed. This we know. Michael survived. The parents were killed in Yugoslavia. Michael was with his parents when they were shot in the woods. He fell down. His mother fell over him. He crawled away from his mother, and hid in the woods until he was found by some peasants. And, eventually, he was smuggled in to Italy, where he joined some kind of a children's transport and ended up in Israel. He and my cousin Thea, who was in Israel already at the time, were united at this kibbutz. And she kind of adopted him, because she knew he was one of the family, and took care of him in Israel. He is married and has children of his own now who are in their 20s and late teens. And Thea is, you know, living in Israel. Thea has gone back to Germany a great deal. There is another cousin of mine who survived in Germany. Her name is Annemarie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Annemarie what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Annemarie Wolf. But she is married to-- she was married to a man by the name of Borkowsky, a doctor. Tell you that story. My father's oldest brother, Sigmund, married a Catholic woman-- was a mixed marriage. He died in 1928. My uncle died in 1928. His wife remarried a Catholic man. Annemarie, who is my uncle's daughter, my first cousin, was raised as a Catholic. And her mother had to swear to the Nazis that she was conceived out of wedlock, because the records show that, at her birth, she was still married to a Jewish man. And so she said, you know, she had an affair with somebody else and that's how this child was born. And that's how Annemarie survived. But she was very conscious always of her Jewishness. And she went to Israel years before I did. She spoke Hebrew as the local people do. She studied Hebrew from that time on, after the war. Her children are very-- also very conscious of what their heritage is and who their grandfather was. So I didn't know-- I knew Annemarie when I was a child. She was one of the cousins. But I had lost all contact with her, until I went back to Germany with my husband in 1970. That was the big trip. I hadn't gone back to Europe until then. And I looked up Annemarie, because I knew she was living in Cologne. And we had this wonderful reunion. We knew we were cousins. It was, you know, it's like blood is thicker than water. And we were very close. Unfortunately, she died of liver cancer in 1984. And-- but the children-- amazingly-- her children and my children-- have become very good friends. They're second cousins, of course. But her children all speak English. They live in Europe. And they come to the United States very often. They get together with my children. My son, who, when he was working for IBM, used to travel a great deal to Germany, and was very close to the cousins-- visited them all the time. And it was nice to pick up that remnant of relatives that survived-- survived in Germany actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have an enormous family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except, of course, in my father's-- of my father's siblings, of all nine children, one died in the First World War, as a soldier in the German army. His oldest sister survived in Sao Paulo in Brazil. And he survived in New York. All the others were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your father's sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mila. Tante Mila.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-I-L-A. And her last-- no, well, her-- her maiden name was Wolf, but she married a man by the name of Moritz Marx. And the children there are living in Brazil now are Marx's.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, just so that we know where you went after-- you came to the United States. You went to school. Did you go to college here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go? What was your profession after?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. I went to school here. Eventually, I went to the high school-- what is now the High School of Art and Design. And I became a designer of children's wear, until my second son was born. And then I moved on to teaching, because I felt that designing was too much of a career for someone who wanted to be a wife and a mother. And I became a teacher. And I started to work at the Fashion Institute of Technology, where, eventually, I became a professor. And then, I was in charge of the fashion design department. Then I was a dean, until 1987. In '92-- between '87 and '92, I went back to teaching, but then I resigned and-- retired, actually, in 1992. I went to college after my second child was born, when I decided that I wanted to be a teacher. So I went to Queens College in my 30s as a part-time student. It took me 10 years, but I got my B.A, before my children actually were ready for college. And that was always my goal. I said, I must be out of school before they get into college. So I did that. Later on, I wrote two textbooks that are still being used. And I'm happy about that. And so I've made my contribution in fashion education.
